title: Magneto Transport and Spin Reorientation in Pt Co78Ho22 Heterostructures Near the Sublattice Compensation Temperature
authors: Rajeev Nepal, Jose Flores, Aurain Seaton, Michael Newburger, John Derek Demaree, Ramesh C Budhani
abstract: Metallic amorphous ferrimagnets of 3d transition metals (TM) and rare earths (RE) with 4f electrons exhibit rich magneto transport behavior due to the interplay between the 3d and 4f magnetic sublattices and their interaction with mobile charges. Tuning the TM and RE concentrations in the alloy can effectively modulate the compensation temperature, where the moments of the two sublattices point in opposite direction leading to a net zero magnetization. Despite extensive magnetotransport studies in Gd and Tb based 3d 4f systems, Ho based alloys remain comparatively underexplored, even though Ho possesses the largest orbital angular momentum (OAM) among the lanthanides. This unquenched OAM can strongly impact magnetic anisotropy and magnetotransport in ferrimagnetic heterostructures. Here, we have investigated the anomalous Hall resistivity , dc magnetization, and spin Hall magnetoresistance (SHMR) of Co78Ho22.Al film and a Pt.Co78Ho22.Al heterostructure deposited using multitarget magnetron sputtering. The Hall resistivity of both systems shows a distinct sign reversal and prominent wing-shaped hysteresis loops in the vicinity of the compensation temperature (Tcomp), which is accompanied by the minimum saturation magnetization near Tcomp. Furthermore, the SHMR in Pt.Co78Ho22.Al film is enhanced due to the Pt layer. These HM interface-induced prominent features of magneto-transport are addressed in the light of the existing theories of spin flop transitions, spin orbit torque, and microscopic phase separation, which may lead to the formation of 3d and 4f magnetic clusters in the film.
summary: This paper explores how adding a platinum layer to cobalt-holmium ferrimagnetic films modifies their magnetic and transport properties near the temperature where sublattices cancel out. It demonstrates that the platinum interface enhances spin-transport effects and identifies a specific magnetic transition (spin-flop) that causes complex switching behavior. This work is significant for developing low-moment spintronic devices.
main_problem: Investigating the magneto-transport anomalies, spin-reorientation transitions, and the impact of a Pt interface on the magnetic properties of Ho-based ferrimagnetic heterostructures near the sublattice compensation temperature.
main_result: The Pt underlayer shifts the compensation temperature downward, enhances net magnetization via proximity effects, and amplifies spin Hall magnetoresistance (SMR) while suppressing orbital magnetoresistance (OMR). The study also identifies an intrinsic field-induced spin-flop transition as the origin of triple-loop hysteresis in the anomalous Hall effect.
method: DC magnetron co-sputtering was used to fabricate Pt/CoHo heterostructures, followed by integrated measurements of DC magnetization, anomalous Hall effect (AHE), spin Hall magnetoresistance (SMR), and Brillouin light scattering (BLS). Structural characterization included RBS, XRD, XRR, and AFM.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of metallic amorphous ferrimagnetic Co78Ho22 thin films and Pt/Co78Ho22 heterostructures, characterized by two antiferromagnetically coupled sublattices (3d Co and 4f Ho).
key_observables: Anomalous Hall resistivity (AHE), saturation magnetization (Ms), spin Hall magnetoresistance (SMR), orbital magnetoresistance (OMR), and spin-wave modes via BLS.
important_parameters: Sublattice compensation temperature (Tcomp), spin-flop field (Hsf), and the orbital angular momentum (OAM) of Holmium.
main_assumptions: The study considers the possibility of microscopic phase separation or magnetic clusters, though it ultimately argues for an intrinsic spin-flop mechanism.
figures_summary: Figures show the evolution of AHE hysteresis loops into triple-loop structures near Tcomp, the enhancement of magnetization in Pt/HoCo vs bare HoCo, the temperature-dependent shift of Tcomp, and the frequency-dependent spin-wave modes in BLS.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the importance of Ho-based alloys, details the fabrication and structural characterization, presents magnetometry and transport results (AHE, SMR, OMR) across temperature ranges, discusses the role of the Pt interface and proximity effects, and concludes with the identification of the spin-flop transition mechanism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Topographical and magnetic textures of a 25 nm HoCo film deposited on a 9 nm Pt underlayer on an amorphous quartz substrate. (a) XRR profile with pronounced interference fringes, indicating a smooth, continuous film of sharp interfaces. (b) XRD pattern showing a broad amorphous background from the quartz substrate and weak Pt (111) reflection. (c) AFM image showing a smooth surface with fine nanoscale texture with an average roughness of  0.86 nm. (d) MFM image revealing a dense labyrinthine domain pattern, characteristic of out-of-plane magnetic anisotropy in ferrimagnetic films.

Figure 2: Magnetization and Hall resistivity measurements on HoCo film and HoCo/Pt bilayer, with the magnetic field applied perpendicular to the film plane (out-of-plane). (a) Hall resistivity ρxy as a function of temperature of the bare 25 nm thick HoCo film measured at 2 tesla, showing a polarity reversal at the magnetic compensation temperature (Tcomp) ~ 192 K. All ρxy data have been antisymmetrized to isolate the odd Hall component. (b) Temperature dependence of the saturation magnetization (Ms) and the magnetization reversal field (Hrev) for the HoCo film, with Ms nearly vanishing at Tcomp. (c) ρxy of the HoCo/Pt bilayer at 2 tesla, exhibiting a sign reversal at ~ 135 K. (d)...

Figure 3: (a) Hall resistivity (ρₓᵧ) of HoCo film over a temperature range of 150 to 250 K. A dramatic change in the shape of the xy loops, along with a sign reversal are seen in the figure. A distinct triple-loop (pointed by the red arrow) in the xy (H) is observed near the compensation temperature of Tcomp  190 K. (b) Corresponding out-of-plane magnetization (M–H) loops of the same sample at T = 150, 180, 190, 200, and 220 K. The red arrow in Fig. 3(b) is suggestive of a metamagnetic spin-flop (SF) transition. (c) Comparison of positive field branches (0 to +5 T) of ρₓᵧ and M(H) loops at T = 190 K, highlighting the difference in magnetization reversal behavior observed in transport and...
